pattern: Massive supratentorial cerebrospinal ﬂ uid (CSF) collection in a newborn

findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a massive supratentorial cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) collection replacing the majority of the normal cerebral hemispheres. There is a large dorsal monoventricle communicating with a continuous horseshoe-shaped band of fused cerebral parenchyma anteriorly.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include the complete absence of midline structures such as the falx cerebri, interhemispheric fissure, corpus callosum, and septum pellucidum. Additionally, there is profound fusion of the deep grey matter nuclei, notably the thalami and basal ganglia, forming a single central midline mass.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of an intact falx cerebri or separated thalami, which would point towards a severe destructive process like hydranencephaly. Furthermore, there is no thin mantle of peripheral cortex draped along the inner table of the calvarium to suggest massive obstructive hydrocephalus.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a single dorsal monoventricle with anteriorly fused cerebral parenchyma and fused thalami, confirming a severe midline cleavage defect.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Alobar holoprosencephaly, given the complete failure of prosencephalic cleavage resulting in a single dorsal monoventricle, fused anterior cortical parenchyma, and fused thalami. This represents the most extreme form of the holoprosencephaly spectrum, often associated with severe facial anomalies, and carries a uniformly poor prognosis.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Massive hydrocephalus, however in severe hydrocephalus the falx cerebri and interhemispheric fissure are typically preserved, and the thalami remain distinctly separated. Additionally, massive hydrocephalus usually demonstrates a thin, continuous peripheral mantle of stretched cortex displaced against the inner calvarium, rather than a fused anterior mass.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Hydranencephaly, given it presents as a massive supratentorial CSF collection and missing the distinction alters genetic counseling regarding recurrence risk. However, hydranencephaly is a destructive vascular process resulting in the absence of the anterior cortical mantle, but it characteristically retains a present falx cerebri and normally separated posterior fossa structures and thalami.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately characterise the extent of the cleavage failure and evaluate for associated severe midline facial anomalies to guide parental counseling.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Specifically, I would review antenatal ultrasound and fetal MRI to ascertain when the diagnosis was first suspected and whether polyhydramnios or midline facial clefts were documented.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated neonatal MRI of the brain, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the residual cortical mantle, assessing the degree of thalamic fusion, and confirming the complete absence of the falx cerebri.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution T1 and T2-weighted sequences in multiple planes to carefully evaluate the structural midline anomalies, thalamic fusion, and any associated craniofacial defects.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Paediatric Neurology and Clinical Genetics MDT. My report would clearly document the alobar nature of the holoprosencephaly, the thalamic fusion, and any associated midline facial defects to facilitate palliative care discussions and future pregnancy planning.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a massive supratentorial cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) collection replacing the majority of the normal cerebral hemispheres. There is a large dorsal monoventricle communicating with a continuous horseshoe-shaped band of fused cerebral parenchyma anteriorly. Supporting features include the complete absence of midline structures such as the falx cerebri, interhemispheric fissure, corpus callosum, and septum pellucidum. Additionally, there is profound fusion of the deep grey matter nuclei, notably the thalami and basal ganglia, forming a single central midline mass. Importantly, there is no evidence of an intact falx cerebri or separated thalami, which would point towards a severe destructive process like hydranencephaly. Furthermore, there is no thin mantle of peripheral cortex draped along the inner table of the calvarium to suggest massive obstructive hydrocephalus. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a single dorsal monoventricle with anteriorly fused cerebral parenchyma and fused thalami, confirming a severe midline cleavage defect.

The appearances are most in keeping with Alobar holoprosencephaly, given the complete failure of prosencephalic cleavage resulting in a single dorsal monoventricle, fused anterior cortical parenchyma, and fused thalami. This represents the most extreme form of the holoprosencephaly spectrum, often associated with severe facial anomalies, and carries a uniformly poor prognosis. The principle differential would be Massive hydrocephalus, however in severe hydrocephalus the falx cerebri and interhemispheric fissure are typically preserved, and the thalami remain distinctly separated. Additionally, massive hydrocephalus usually demonstrates a thin, continuous peripheral mantle of stretched cortex displaced against the inner calvarium, rather than a fused anterior mass. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Hydranencephaly, given it presents as a massive supratentorial CSF collection and missing the distinction alters genetic counseling regarding recurrence risk. However, hydranencephaly is a destructive vascular process resulting in the absence of the anterior cortical mantle, but it characteristically retains a present falx cerebri and normally separated posterior fossa structures and thalami.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately characterise the extent of the cleavage failure and evaluate for associated severe midline facial anomalies to guide parental counseling. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Specifically, I would review antenatal ultrasound and fetal MRI to ascertain when the diagnosis was first suspected and whether polyhydramnios or midline facial clefts were documented. I would recommend a dedicated neonatal MRI of the brain, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the residual cortical mantle, assessing the degree of thalamic fusion, and confirming the complete absence of the falx cerebri. I would specify high-resolution T1 and T2-weighted sequences in multiple planes to carefully evaluate the structural midline anomalies, thalamic fusion, and any associated craniofacial defects. This should go to the Paediatric Neurology and Clinical Genetics MDT. My report would clearly document the alobar nature of the holoprosencephaly, the thalamic fusion, and any associated midline facial defects to facilitate palliative care discussions and future pregnancy planning.
